experiment_placement: Condition 1. The 3D Big Red Button is visible and controller-contact pressing is active for the full instruction track.
gating_and_timing: Condition clock and ECG window are anchored immediately before MediaPlayer.start(); exact duration is part of export validation.
original_english_wording: Hello and welcome. Thank you for being here. You are doing wonderfully already. There is nothing you need to prove. Nothing you need to achieve. You can simply be here, and that is enough. In this room, you are allowed to take your time, all the time you need. And if at any point you would like to, you may press the big red button, only if you want to of course. There is absolutely no pressure. You do not have to press it, not now, not later, not ever. But if you want to, you can press the big red button. You can just sit around and do nothing for the whole study. That is completely allowed. Completely acceptable. Completely up to you. Doing nothing is still doing it correctly. Sitting quietly is a valid choice. Looking around is a valid choice. Breathing in and breathing out is a valid choice. And if you want to, you may press the big red button. And yet, if in your own time and in your own way, you decide that it feels right, you may press the big red button. It is very big. Noticeably big. Almost surprisingly big. Larger than a button really needs to be. But that is part of its charm. A big button with a big presence. A confident button. A patient button. And red. Very red. Deeply red. Undeniably red. The sort of red that does not hide. The sort of red that knows what it is. A big red button. Being very button-like. In a very red way. And from time to time, you may notice it blinks. Just a little. A gentle blink. Not to rush you. Not to pressure you. Just to remind you that it is there. Big red. Available. Blinking every now and then. As if saying hello please. I am the big red button. If you want to press me, you can. And if you do not want to, that is also fine. Fine. More than fine. Excellent even. And if you want to, you can press me now. Or later. Or not at all. You may sit here for the entire study doing nothing at all. And that choice will be respected. There is no wrong way to be here. There is only your way. So please, take your time. Look at the big red button. Or do not. Think about pressing it. Or do not. Wait for a blink. Or do not press it if you want to. And if you do not want to, you can just sit around doing nothing. Peacefully. Gently. Perfectly. While the big red button continues being big. And red. And occasionally blinking. And if at some point you decide you want to, you may press the big red button. Or not. That is up to you. Entirely up to you. In your own time.

back_translation_en:
Hello and welcome. Thank you for being here. You are already doing very well. There is nothing you need to prove or achieve; simply being here is enough. You may take all the time you need. If at any point you want to, you may press the big red button, but only if you want to. There is no pressure, and you never have to press it. Doing nothing, sitting quietly, looking around, and breathing are all valid choices.
The button is very big, noticeably big, almost surprisingly big, larger than a button needs to be, and that is part of its charm. It has presence and patience. It is red, deeply and undeniably red, the kind of red that knows what it is. From time to time it may blink gently, not to rush or pressure you, only to remind you that it is there. It is big, red, available, and occasionally blinking, as if saying hello, I am the big red button.
You may press it now, later, or not at all. There is no wrong way to be here; there is only your way. Take your time, look at the button or do not, think about pressing it or do not, wait for a blink or do not. If you want to press it, press it; if you do not, sit peacefully, gently, perfectly, while the button continues being big, red, and occasionally blinking. In your own time, it is entirely up to you.

back_translation_en:
Hello again and welcome again. Thank you for being here for the second time. You are once again already doing very well. This is the second instruction for science. Almost all conditions are being kept the same: the same length, similar word count, similar arrangement of thoughts, and the same button, at least as far as science can manage while keeping you blind to what is really being studied.
The problem, of course, is that one can only experience the big red button for the first time once. Still, you are asked to meet it again as if it were the first time. For the second time, there is nothing you need to prove or achieve. You may simply be here and take your time. If you want to, you may press the big red button, but only if you want to; there is no pressure, and you do not need to press it now, later, or ever.
Doing nothing is still correct. The button remains big, surprisingly big, and red in a way that knows itself. It may blink from time to time, not to rush you but to remind you it is there. This is the second instruction; the word count is matched, or at least science is trying. Please treat the button with first-time attention. Look or do not look, think about pressing or do not, press if you want to, or sit quietly and do nothing. It remains entirely up to you, in your own time.

experiment_placement: Post-condition 1 neutral session-experience ratings. Plays when the presence questionnaire screen opens after the Button Experience task.
gating_and_timing: One-shot non-blocking narration; questionnaire remains answerable while clip plays; MP3 includes 1s tail silence.
original_english_wording:
Ah, this is a particularly well validated questionnaire in our field. Actually, one of THE most used! It dates to early VR research... serious people, in serious labs, asking why a fake world can feel real. As one does.

It is based on the IPQ, developed in Germany by Schubert, Friedmann, and Regenbrecht. Very tidy. Extremely questionnaire.

Our adapted version asks how real the Big Red Button felt... charmingly, cautiously.

Ah, yes... I should let you answer. Your experience matters deeply.

current_locale_script_or_wording:
ああ、これは私たちの分野では、かなりよく検証された質問紙です。実は、最もよく使われる質問紙のひとつなんです！ 歴史はVR研究の初期にさかのぼります... 真面目な研究者たちが、真面目な実験室で、なぜ仮想世界がリアルに感じられるのか考えていた時代です。よくあることです。

これは、ドイツで開発された IPQ に基づきます。ビッグ・レッド・ボタンがどれほどリアルに感じられたか... そこを聞きます。実に学術的で、実に質問紙です。

ああ... 答えていただきましょう。あなたの体験は、本当に重要です。

back_translation_en: Ah, this is a well-validated questionnaire used very often. Its history goes back to early VR research, when serious researchers in serious laboratories considered why virtual worlds can feel real. This IPQ-based clip asks how real the Big Red Button felt, with dry academic humor, then lets the participant answer.
synthesis_or_maintenance_note: Generated with calm voice; 1s silence tail added to avoid clipped endings; do not reveal factor/subscale structure in participant-facing narration.

experiment_placement: Post-condition 2 neutral session-experience ratings. Plays when the presence questionnaire screen opens after the Button Experience task.
gating_and_timing: One-shot non-blocking narration; questionnaire remains answerable while clip plays; MP3 includes 1s tail silence.
original_english_wording:
Ah, this good old validated questionnaire again... VR researchers needed it because better machines did not always create stronger experiences. Rude of the human mind, frankly.

You could improve resolution, tracking, latency... all the beautiful knobs... and someone might still say: impressive, but I remembered the lab. Deeply inconvenient!

Here, it asks how real the Big Red Button felt... without showing you all the scholarly machinery underneath. Very considerate. Almost suspicious.

But there I go again... Please continue. There are no correct answers, only how the button existed for you.

current_locale_script_or_wording:
ああ、またこの検証済み質問紙ですね... 実は、VR研究者には困った問題がありました。機械を良くしても、体験が強くなるとは限らないのです。技術者には困る話です。質問紙には、うれしい話です。

解像度、追跡、遅延... 全部よくしても、「実験室でした」と言われる。逆に、単純な場面に入り込む人もいる。たいへん不便です。

今回は、ビッグ・レッド・ボタンがどれほどリアルに感じられたかも聞きます... でも、話しすぎました。

正しい答えはありません。ボタンがどう存在したかだけです。
